attributes.stem:
A 34-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a five-month history of low mood and difficulty sleeping. He explains that his symptoms began shortly after his partner of four years ended their relationship. He reports low motivation, significant teariness, and withdrawal from his normal hobbies including playing cricket on weekends. Despite these symptoms, he is still able to function well at his job as an engineer and complete his normal daily activities. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears sad but uses appropriate body language and is reactive in affect. His vital signs are within normal limits. He has difficulty falling asleep due to 'racing thoughts' about his ex-partner, although once asleep, he sleeps through the night without problems. There have been no changes in weight, appetite, concentration, or memory. Although sad, he denies feelings of worthlessness or guilt. He denies any suicidal ideation or planning.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following diagnoses best describes this presentation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with emotional and behavioural symptoms that developed following an identifiable stressor (the end of a four-year relationship). The symptoms have persisted for five months and include low mood, teariness, sleep difficulties, and social withdrawal. Despite these symptoms, he maintains good occupational functioning and does not meet the full criteria for major depression.

Adjustment disorder is the most appropriate diagnosis. This condition is characterised by emotional or behavioural symptoms that develop in response to an identifiable stressor, are clinically significant (causing distress or functional impairment), and are out of proportion to what would be expected. The symptoms typically begin within three months of the stressor and usually resolve within six months after the stressor ends. This patient's five-month duration of symptoms, preservation of function at work, and clear temporal relationship to the relationship breakdown all support this diagnosis.

Major depressive episode requires at least five symptoms present for at least two weeks, including either depressed mood or anhedonia, plus at least four additional symptoms from: significant weight/appetite change, insomnia or hypersomnia, psychomotor agitation or retardation, fatigue or loss of energy, feelings of worthlessness or excessive guilt, diminished concentration or indecisiveness, and recurrent thoughts of death or suicidal ideation. This patient has only three symptoms (low mood, anhedonia, and initial insomnia), which is insufficient for this diagnosis.

Acute stress disorder requires exposure to a qualifying traumatic event (e.g., serious injury), either directly or indirectly, according to the DSM-5 diagnostic criteria. The disorder involves specific symptom clusters, including intrusion symptoms (recurrent distressing memories, nightmares, flashbacks), avoidance of trauma-related stimuli, negative alterations in cognition and mood, alterations in arousal and reactivity, and often dissociative symptoms. Additionally, symptoms must last between three days and one month after trauma exposure. This patient does not report exposure to a qualifying traumatic event, lacks the characteristic symptom clusters, and has symptoms lasting five months.

Generalised anxiety disorder is characterised by excessive anxiety and worry about various events or activities, occurring more days than not for at least six months. The worry must be difficult to control and associated with at least three additional symptoms such as restlessness, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, irritability, muscle tension, or sleep disturbance. While this patient reports 'racing thoughts' at bedtime, there is no evidence of pervasive, uncontrollable worry about multiple domains of life.

Normal stress reaction would be expected following the end of a significant relationship and might include temporary low mood, sleep changes, and social withdrawal. However, the five-month duration and ongoing functional impairment in social activities suggest this response exceeds what would be considered normal grief or adjustment to loss.
learningPoints: Adjustment disorder is diagnosed when emotional or behavioural symptoms develop in response to an identifiable stressor and are out of proportion to the expected reaction.

Major depressive episode requires at least five symptoms including either depressed mood or anhedonia, plus four other symptoms such as sleep disturbance, appetite/weight changes, or suicidal ideation.
The duration of symptoms is important in differentiating adjustment disorder (typically resolves within six months) from major depression.
references: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders: DSM-5. 5th ed., American Psychiatric Association, 2013. DSM-V.

Geer K. Adjustment Disorder: Diagnosis and Treatment in Primary Care. Prim Care. 2023 Mar;50(1):83-88. doi: 10.1016/j.pop.2022.10.006. Epub 2023 Jan 10. PMID: 36822730. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36822730/

O'Donnell ML, Agathos JA, Metcalf O, Gibson K, Lau W. Adjustment Disorder: Current Developments and Future Directions. Int J Environ Res Public Health. 2019 Jul 16;16(14):2537. doi: 10.3390/ijerph16142537. PMID: 31315203; PMCID: PMC6678970. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6678970/

Administration SA and MHS. Table 3.30, DSM-IV to DSM-5 Acute Stress Disorder Comparison [Internet]. Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (US); 2016 [cited 2025 Sept 7]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK519704/table/ch3.t30/
